questions.scenario:
The Node.js config helper contains this default:

DATABASE_URL = process.env.DATABASE_URL || "postgres://localhost/orders"

```text
Production logs show:
WARN DATABASE_URL missing, using default localhost
ERROR connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:5432
```

What is the best fix?

options.text: Allow the localhost default in each environment, but add a metric when production uses it.
Fail startup clearly when required production DATABASE_URL is missing instead of using a quiet default.
Use a non-secret production endpoint as the default and keep credentials in a second variable.
Make `DATABASE_URL` required in production and permit the local fallback only in an explicitly selected development environment.

questions.explanation:
Defaults are fine only when the fallback is genuinely safe. A production database URL is required config, so missing should stop startup with a clear message. Quiet defaults turn config mistakes into confusing runtime failures.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS task definitions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_definitions.html).

questions.scenario:
A deployment log shows:

```text
INFO boot service=devpolaris-orders-api release=2026-05-02.6
WARN DATABASE_URL missing, continuing startup
INFO http listening address=0.0.0.0 port=3000
ERROR POST /v1/orders status=500 reason=no_database_config
```

What should change?

options.text: Keep listening and return unhealthy readiness indefinitely, even though the missing value cannot arrive without a new task definition.
Validate only when the first checkout request arrives so deployment health can complete before production traffic.
Validate required config during startup and fail before serving traffic when DATABASE_URL is missing.
Let the target group reject requests while the service polls for configuration because ECS will eventually replace unhealthy tasks.

questions.explanation:
Required production config is part of the startup contract. Failing fast with a clear missing-name message is kinder than letting the app accept traffic and fail orders. The log should name the missing variable without printing secret values.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS task definitions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_definitions.html).

questions.scenario:
A DevPolaris release captain is validating task definition 39 before using it as the production rollback target. Its secret reference is:

DATABASE_URL -> arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:111122223333:secret:prod/orders/old-database-url-a1b2

Secrets Manager metadata says: Old-database-url-a1b2 is DeletedDate 2026-04-28T11:00:00Z.

What should the team consider before rollback?

options.text: Rollback to revision 39 after recreating a secret with the same friendly name, without checking the full ARN stored in the revision.
Validate the deleted secret reference only, assuming the rest of revision 39 remains compatible with current dependencies.
Revision 39 may not be a safe rollback target because its secret reference no longer exists.
Create revision 40 with current secret references and call it the rollback target before testing startup and target health.

questions.explanation:
Rollback restores a runtime recipe, and that recipe must still be compatible with current config and secrets. A deleted secret reference can make the rollback fail during initialization. Check the contract before pointing production back.

Learn more in [AWS Secrets Manager concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/intro.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris rotates the production database secret while ECS tasks remain running. Existing tasks received the secret through task-definition secret injection at startup, and a new checkout implementation is protected by a feature flag. The team wants to rotate credentials and expose the feature gradually without sending traffic to tasks that still use the old secret. Which three actions belong in the rollout?

options.text: Deploy replacement tasks that resolve the new secret version and prove readiness before old credentials are retired.
Assume existing task environment variables update when Secrets Manager rotates the value, so no task replacement is required.
Keep the feature flag separate from artifact deployment so candidate behavior can be enabled gradually after runtime health is proven.
Maintain a controlled overlap or rollback path for the old credential until replacement tasks and database access are verified.
Print both database passwords in startup logs so operators can compare which tasks refreshed successfully.

questions.explanation:
Secret injection into an ECS task happens at startup, so rotation normally requires replacement tasks to receive the new value. Credential overlap or another controlled transition prevents old tasks from losing access before the new tasks are healthy. A feature flag can then expose candidate behavior independently. Existing environment values do not refresh automatically, and secret values must not be logged.

Learn more in [Passing Secrets Manager secrets to ECS containers](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/secrets-envvar-secrets-manager.html).

questions.scenario:
A task definition review lists:

```text
environment:
  NODE_ENV=production
  PORT=3000
  ORDER_TIMEOUT_MS=2500
  PUBLIC_BASE_URL=https://orders.devpolaris.com
secrets:
  DATABASE_URL from Secrets Manager
  PAYMENT_WEBHOOK_SECRET from SSM Parameter Store
```

Which review comment is best?

options.text: Move each value to Secrets Manager so all runtime configuration shares one retrieval and rotation path.
This split is sensible: ordinary values are visible but intentional, and access-bearing values are referenced as secrets.
Keep credentials in plaintext environment entries because ECS task-definition access is already controlled by IAM.
Bake stable values such as `PORT` and `PUBLIC_BASE_URL` into the image, while referencing only credentials at runtime.

questions.explanation:
The useful distinction is what the value can do. Ordinary config can be visible and still reviewed carefully. Database URLs and webhook secrets grant access or trust, so the task definition should reference them without exposing values.

Learn more in [AWS Secrets Manager concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/intro.html).

questions.scenario:
A healthy startup log says:

```text
INFO config node_env=production port=3000 order_timeout_ms=2500
INFO config secrets_present=DATABASE_URL,PAYMENT_WEBHOOK_SECRET
INFO database=startup_check_ok latency_ms=42
INFO health=ready path=/health
```

Why is this log shape useful?

options.text: Log the names of present secrets and the successful database check, but omit release and ordinary config metadata.
Treat this startup event as sufficient traffic evidence and skip target-health verification.
Treat secret-name presence as proof that the values are current and authorized for each downstream operation.
It proves safe parts of the runtime contract and secret presence without leaking secret values.

questions.explanation:
Good startup logs expose metadata and readiness, not credentials. They tell operators which config names were present and whether a fast dependency check passed. Target health and request metrics still matter after startup.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS task definitions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_definitions.html).

questions.scenario:
An operator is investigating a missing production secret from the DevPolaris runbook. Before changing IAM or secret resources, the operator checks the command context.

Aws sts get-caller-identity Account is 222222222222. Expected production account is 111122223333. Aws region is us-west-2. Orders service region is us-east-1. Error is secret prod/orders/DATABASE_URL not found.

What should happen first?

options.text: Stop and fix the AWS account and Region context before reading deeper secret or ECS evidence.
Assume the secret was deleted because `ResourceNotFoundException` is independent of account and Region.
Switch only the Region first and leave the account mismatch for the role-permission investigation.
Correct the Region but continue using account `222222222222` because the secret name matches production.

questions.explanation:
Wrong workspace evidence is a hard stop. Many secret-not-found errors are just account or Region mistakes. Fix the context before changing production resources or permissions.

Learn more in [AWS STS GetCallerIdentity](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_GetCallerIdentity.html).
